comics.title: DHCP Friday
comics.alt: A sysadmin contemplates making a DHCP change on a Friday afternoon. Ten minutes later, every device loses connectivity. The sysadmin — now in sunglasses, watching 192.168.1.1 go DOWN — remains unbothered. He has simply moved beyond fear.

comics.title: Bring Your Own Jar
comics.alt: A person correctly predicts that any event requiring you to bring your own jar will be a good time. They attend Fermentation Fun Night. They were right.

comics.title: The Alphanumeric Shishkebab
comics.alt: UUIDs are technically correct but illegible to humans. A Pictographic Art Hash (PAH) maps any identifier to a short sequence of emoji — deterministic, compact, and scannable at a glance. The Cool Sysadmin closes the case: UUIDs for computers, art hashes for humans. Ship it.

comics.title: 613-GET-HELP
comics.alt: An emergency runbook for the server room: don't panic, just call Aaron. A flowchart determines whether Aaron is on vacation or already fixing it. Either way, you don't need to do anything. The Cool Sysadmin is already in the server room, feet up, unbothered.

comics.title: FLOOMP
comics.alt: A stick figure goes to see Epic Space Saga, buys a large popcorn, then encounters a giant overstuffed recliner seat in the theater. They sit down with a FLOOMP and sink progressively deeper into the cushions until nearly swallowed. The film plays while the character is barely visible above the upholstery. Afterwards, standing outside, they reflect that it was fine but they're not a fan of the oversized seats. A lobby poster advertises the next upgrade: Even More Cushion — MAX.

comics.title: Worth it
comics.alt: A presenter proposes a simple expected-value calculation: take a one-in-three chance of ending human existence in exchange for a two-thirds chance of dramatically raising living standards by a factor of fifty-five. The math is laid out on a whiteboard with bureaucratic calm. The committee votes to roll the cosmic dice. The joke is that existential annihilation has been compressed into tidy optimization math.

variants.seed:
"It is optimal to take a 1 in 3 chance of ending human existence in exchange
for a 2/3 chance of dramatically raising living standards by a factor of 55"

comics.title: Free!
comics.alt: A sidewalk sign reads FREE SPIDER PLANTS. A passerby takes one — they're easy, hard to kill. The owner explains that once you have one spider plant, it inevitably becomes dozens, then overwhelms the home entirely. The owner, lost in a jungle of spider plants, desperately tries to give them away and eventually sets up their own FREE SPIDER PLANTS sign. The circle of chlorophyll. You can check out any time, but you can never leaf.

comics.title: Unconventional
comics.alt: I would like to wish for less wishes.

variants.prompt:
Black-and-white webcomic in a minimalist hand-drawn style. Simple stick figures: a circle for the head connected to a vertical line for the body, with straight lines for arms and legs. No filled bodies, no clothing, no shading on characters. Bold, consistent ink line weight throughout — lines should be clearly visible, not faint. Every stick figure head must have a face: two small dot eyes and a simple curved mouth showing the character's expression (smile, frown, flat line, raised eyebrow). Faces are mandatory — never draw a blank circle for a head. Bold black ink linework on a plain white background. No color. Use hatching and cross-hatching to convey shadows, depth, and material texture. Hand-lettered text in plain speech bubbles and rectangular caption boxes. All text must be perfectly legible real English words — no garbled letters, no nonsense strings. Dry, witty, technically-aware humor. Sparse backgrounds — props, labels, and small signs carry the context and the jokes. Every mug, whiteboard, label, badge, sticky note, or piece of paraphernalia must display a short readable dry-wit slogan directly related to the scene (e.g. 'git blame', 'works on my machine', 'sudo make coffee', 'undefined behaviour', '404: sleep not found'). Draw props with enough detail for the text to be legible. Generous interior margins: all content must sit well inside the image, with at least 18% clear space from every outer edge. Nothing should touch or run off an edge.

Render each panel independently. The browser will stitch the 3 finished panels into one horizontal strip.

Draw each panel exactly as described below. All text must be perfectly legible.

Panel 1: A stick figure holding a stereotypical magic lamp, a cloud of smoke emerging from the spout. Text: "Oh powerful genie, I wish for less wishes."
Panel 2: The genie, a vague humanoid form in the smoke, pauses and looks puzzled.
Panel 3: A pop-up appears out of thin air: 'Error: Wish Syntax Invalid.'
variants.seed: I would like to wish for less wishes.

comics.title: lobot-tui: The Terminal That Notices Everything
Extra Hours, Lost in Smoke
comics.alt: We only get a few extra hours of sun in the summer. Sucks that we have national forest fires blocking the sky. Not technical. Human observations and complaints.

comics.title: One Port, Many Consequences
comics.alt: A public site that previously needed exceptions on both the campus external

comics.title: Too early for Christmas greats?
comics.alt: The Chipmunk Song (Christmas Don't Be Late)

variants.prompt:
Human-Readable house style: a mostly monochrome, hand-drawn editorial webcomic. The recurring protagonist is a minimalist stick figure with a round head, dot eyes, and a consistent silhouette. Backgrounds are detailed and grounded; characters remain deliberately simple. Use clean black ink, restrained hatching, and at most one warm orange accent color. Dialogue is short, legible, and hand-lettered in plain speech bubbles. The humor is dry, observational, technically aware, and never explained.

A single panel.

Tell this idea as a short comic, inventing the beats and any dialogue:

"The Chipmunk Song (Christmas Don't Be Late)
(Written by Ross Bagdasarian Sr. Recorded by the Chipmunks 1958.)
(G) (D7)(G)(D7)(G)(D7)(G)(D7)
(G) Christmas, (G6) Christmas (D7) time is near Time for toys and (Gmaj7) time for cheer
(Am7) We've been (D7) good but (Am7) we can't (D7) last (Am7) Hurry (D7) Christmas, (G) hurry (G6) fast
(G) Want a (G6) plane that (Gmaj7) loops the (G6) loop
(G7) Me, I want a (C) hula hoop
(C) We can (Cm) hardly (G) stand the (E7) wait
Please (Am7) Christmas, (D7) doe
• (G) late"

Land the ending softly with a realization, a label, or an understated escalation.

Do not explain the joke in text.
